images.caption: Figure 1:A sketched overview of the evolutionary landscape in the paradigms of multimodal modeling. In the final stage, the model achieves a born-native state where all modalities are processed within a unified transformer space, facilitating symmetric multi-to-multi understanding and generation.

Figure 2:Evolutionary timeline and functional taxonomy of Native Multimodal Foundation Models (2023–2026). The upward trajectory charts the historical progression from early mid-fusion alignment to the early-fusion methods, i.e., born-native transformer.
Figure 3:Illustrative examples of three primary NMM architectures considering input-output duality.

Figure 5:Training×\timesFusion: a stage-by-regime grid.Rows = training stages (PT/SFT/RL/OPD); columns = fusion regimes (late/mid/early).Every rightward arrow is an architectural necessity, not a stylistic choice.1PT late→\rightarrowmid: differential LR becomes mandatory once the encoder receives gradients.2PT mid→\rightarrowearly: z-loss and QK-Norm become preconditions; the modality-mixture schedule replaces differential LR.3RL mid→\rightarrowearly: pathway-locality collapses, so the policy must cover the full backbone—and the three failure modes on the right (grounding hack, see-saw, perception/logic gap) co-emergebecause ofthis collapse, not alongside it.4RL→\rightarrowOPD: teacher-anchored on-policy distillation (bottom bar, fusion-agnostic) is the structural response to3, combining a specialist teacher pool, a hybrid OPD++ORM advantage, and a self-snapshot anchor against drift.
